comment_text.sn17.35:1.1: This discourse is also at AN 4.68; the verse is at SN 6.12. This Sutta and the next (with sequence reversed) form part of the full story of Devadatta in the Vinaya (Kd 17:2.1.21.

root_text.sn17.35:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati gijjhakūṭe pabbate acirapakkante devadatte.
root_text.sn17.35:1.2: Tatra kho bhagavā devadattaṁ ārabbha bhikkhū āmantesi:
root_text.sn17.35:1.3: “attavadhāya, bhikkhave, devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi, parābhavāya devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi.
root_text.sn17.35:2.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, kadalī attavadhāya phalaṁ deti, parābhavāya phalaṁ deti;
root_text.sn17.35:2.2: evameva kho, bhikkhave, attavadhāya devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi, parābhavāya devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi.
root_text.sn17.35:3.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, veḷu attavadhāya phalaṁ deti, parābhavāya phalaṁ deti;
root_text.sn17.35:3.2: evameva kho, bhikkhave, attavadhāya devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi, parābhavāya devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi.
root_text.sn17.35:4.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, naḷo attavadhāya phalaṁ deti, parābhavāya phalaṁ deti;
root_text.sn17.35:4.2: evameva kho, bhikkhave, attavadhāya devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi, parābhavāya devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi.
root_text.sn17.35:5.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, assatarī attavadhāya gabbhaṁ gaṇhāti, parābhavāya gabbhaṁ gaṇhāti;
root_text.sn17.35:5.2: evameva kho, bhikkhave, attavadhāya devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi, parābhavāya devadattassa lābhasakkārasiloko udapādi.
root_text.sn17.35:6.2: Idaṁ vatvāna sugato athāparaṁ etadavoca satthā:

translation_text.sn17.35:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain, not long after Devadatta had left.
translation_text.sn17.35:1.2: There the Buddha spoke to the mendicants about Devadatta:
translation_text.sn17.35:1.3: “Possessions, honor, and popularity came to Devadatta for his own ruin and downfall.
translation_text.sn17.35:4.1: or a reed, all of which bear fruit to their own ruin and downfall …
translation_text.sn17.35:5.1: It’s like a mule, which becomes pregnant to its own ruin and downfall.
translation_text.sn17.35:5.2: In the same way, possessions, honor, and popularity came to Devadatta for his own ruin and downfall.
translation_text.sn17.35:5.3: So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity.
translation_text.sn17.35:6.1: That is what the Buddha said.
translation_text.sn17.35:6.2: Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say:
translation_text.sn17.35:7.1: “The banana plant is destroyed by its own fruit,
translation_text.sn17.35:7.2: as are the bamboo and the reed.
